# Questions in Livestream 110

Live Stream #110: Statues in the Mist

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=338" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">5:38</a> | What is the grammatical function of the prefixed `lamed`? | He explained it as the dative preposition, usually carrying a `to` or `for` sense. | He described Semitic prepositions in Hebrew as often being single letters attached to the front of a noun. In this case the prefixed `lamed` marks a dative construction, indicating motion toward something, relation to a place, or purpose for something. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=481" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">8:01</a> | Could the Torah have been written in Amorite and later updated into Hebrew? | He said yes, that is quite possible given how close Amorite and Hebrew appear to be. | He said the possibility is strong because Amorite appears extremely close to Hebrew in grammar, spelling, and syntax. If an Amorite text used an alphabetic script, later updating it into Hebrew would not require a large linguistic leap, and the newly discussed tablets open interesting questions about Torah textual development. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=655" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">10:55</a> | What was the most interesting piece of political palace intrigue in Egyptian history? | He chose the harem conspiracy against Ramesses III. | He pointed to the harem conspiracy because it involved secondary wives and court officials trying to remove both Ramesses III and the named successor, Ramesses IV, so Pentawere could take the throne. He noted that examination of Ramesses III's mummy indicates the assassination succeeded, but the successor survived and the conspirators were later condemned in court proceedings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=993" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">16:33</a> | Is the mention of iron in Job evidence for an Iron Age date? | He said smelted iron points that way, though the broader ethnic and cultural setting in Job is even stronger evidence. | He distinguished ordinary meteoritic iron, which predates the Iron Age, from the reference to iron being taken from rock and smelted. He still thought the stronger evidence was Job's cultural setting, especially the picture of Aram, Sabeans, and Chaldeans after the breakup of the Late Bronze Age order. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=1244" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">20:44</a> | What did ancient Egyptians call themselves? | He said they generally called themselves `remetch` or class-based terms, not `Kemet` as a self-name in the modern popular sense. | He said Egyptians normally referred to themselves as "the people" and sometimes by class, with an upper stratum called something like the "pat" people. He rejected the popular claim that they called themselves Kemet as a regular ethnic self-name, saying that term is rare in Egyptian documents and that more national labels appear later under wider foreign contact. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=1461" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">24:21</a> | What does he think of the Bible Project? | He praised its accessible big-picture aim but criticized it for sometimes leaning on outdated scholarship. | He said he has seen only some of their material and understands that their purpose is broad, digestible book-by-book summary rather than detailed background work. His main criticism was that they sometimes use dated scholarship, giving the plague-against-Egyptian-gods idea in Exodus as an example, though he otherwise thought the general summary approach was fine. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=1617" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">26:57</a> | What are the benefits of learning Latin, is it useful for Bible study, and are there study tips? | He said Latin is indirectly useful because it teaches grammar that helps with Greek and Hebrew, though he could not offer personal study tips. | He said Latin is especially valuable because it forces students to learn grammar categories such as dative, genitive, accusative, and nominative, which English speakers often have to learn from scratch when studying Greek or Hebrew. He could not give Latin study tips because he never learned it himself, but said its payoff for Bible study comes when students later approach biblical languages. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=1913" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">31:53</a> | Is he familiar with Old Testament scholar Elmer Martin's thoughts? | He said he knew only a little background about Martin and had not read enough of his work to comment on his theology. | He said all he really knew was that Martin had worked at a Mennonite college, and that Martin's work does not commonly appear in the Exodus-related literature he normally studies. Because he had not explored Martin's theology or writings, he declined to evaluate his views. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=1983" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">33:03</a> | Why did God not choose an empty land as the promised land? | He pointed to Exodus 23:29 and said an empty land would become desolate and overrun with animals and thickets before Israel could occupy it. | He read Exodus 23 as saying God would not drive the inhabitants out all at once because the land would become desolate and dangerous animals would multiply. He explained that an empty land would require Israel to clear thorns, thickets, and wildlife before occupying it, making the practical burden too great. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=2263" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">37:43</a> | In Deuteronomy 10:27, does Moses convince God, and is this how prayer works? | He could not answer because Deuteronomy 10 has only 22 verses and asked for a corrected reference. | He stopped at the reference problem rather than trying to infer the intended passage. Since Deuteronomy 10 has only 22 verses, he said he could not locate the text being asked about and asked the questioner to provide the correct reference before he could answer. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=2347" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">39:07</a> | How did the Pharisees interpret the Bible wrongly in Matthew 23:13, and what was the right interpretation? | He said they treated law-keeping as salvific, while the law exposes unrighteousness and points to trust in God through Christ. | He said Matthew 23 alludes to a Second Temple Jewish idea that entrance into the kingdom could come through strict law observance and temple practice. In his reading, the Pharisees missed that the law functions as a schoolmaster, exposing unrighteousness and pointing people to trust God through Christ rather than trusting the law to save. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=2584" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">43:04</a> | Why did God pick Egypt as the place for Joseph, Mary, and Jesus to flee in Matthew 2:13? | He said Egypt had established Jewish communities, was outside Herod's reach, had protected roads, and offered work for Joseph. | He said Egypt was a natural refuge because it had established Torah-observant Jewish communities in places such as Alexandria, Pelusium, Tell el-Yehudiya, and Elephantine. Once outside Judea, the family was beyond Herod's practical power, and Roman Egypt also had protected travel routes and public building work where Joseph's skills could support the family. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=2910" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">48:30</a> | Why did God bring up Job to Satan in Job 1? | He said the text does not give God's reason, but it shows a larger drama under God's control that Job could not fully understand. | He said Job never gives the specific reason God allowed Job to become Satan's target, and that omission is part of the book's point. Job is righteous and does not deserve the suffering, but God's later questioning shows that Job is not in a position to understand all the cosmic dimensions of the drama. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=3341" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">55:41</a> | Does the Old Testament clearly distinguish between veneration and worship? | He said no; that distinction develops much later in church history rather than in the Old Testament. | He said the Old Testament does not provide the later theological distinction between worship as praise and glory and veneration as respect without worship. He placed the clearer development of that distinction in the church era, especially around the rise of icons and related controversies centuries later. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=3540" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">59:00</a> | What is the best way to share the gospel with children, and can children understand sin, heaven, and hell? | He said children can understand at an age-appropriate level, and should be given the biblical story and realistic building blocks for the gospel. | He said children around six or seven can begin to understand sin, heaven, and hell, though a four-year-old may not yet have the same cognitive framework. His advice was to teach the biblical story truthfully, with realistic rather than fantasy-like presentation, so children gain the background pieces needed to understand and respond to the gospel. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=4036" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:07:16</a> | Is there an age of accountability, and does it vary from person to person? | He said Scripture gives no fixed age; accountability depends on what a person has been given and what they can understand, with grace covering those who cannot. | He said the Bible gives no fixed age of accountability and was not persuaded that the wilderness generation's age twenty cutoff establishes one. Instead, he framed accountability in terms of grace and responsibility: those who cannot understand are covered by grace, while those given more understanding are more accountable. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=4288" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:11:28</a> | What is his favorite sitcom? | He said he does not have one because he does not watch sitcoms. | He answered only briefly after returning to the earlier two-part question. He said he has no favorite sitcom because he does not watch sitcoms, then moved on. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=4318" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:11:58</a> | What does he think of Eliakim typological arguments for the papacy? | He was not persuaded, saying the Isaiah 22 and Matthew 16 connection is dubious and that the keys refer to the gospel rather than a papal storehouse of merits. | He described the argument as an apologetic move connecting Isaiah 22's key of David to Matthew 16's keys of the kingdom, but he thought that connection reads too much into the texts. He argued that Matthew's keys concern the preaching of the gospel under Christ's authority, not an exclusive Petrine storehouse of good works or papal authority. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=4745" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:19:05</a> | Does an Aramaic original undercut the Petros/Petra distinction in Matthew 16? | He said exegesis should follow the inspired Greek text received in Matthew, not a hypothetical Aramaic reconstruction. | He acknowledged that the saying may have been spoken in Aramaic, but said interpretation must be based on Matthew's received Greek wording rather than a reconstructed underlying Aramaic text. In that context he reiterated that the keys are the gospel and the authority of Christ, with serious responsibility for those who preach it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=4891" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:21:31</a> | What was the average age for marriage in ancient Near Eastern culture, especially among Israelites? | He said women often married around 15 to 19, men varied more and often married later, and there is no precise average. | He said first marriages were usually arranged by families and reflected survival, fertility, wealth, and future security rather than modern romance. Women might marry in the later teenage years, while men varied widely depending on wealth, inheritance, military obligations, and life circumstances, so any average remains approximate. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=5254" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:27:34</a> | What are good resources, commentaries, or articles on Job? | He could not name specific older resources from memory, but suggested starting with the NICOT and possibly Hermeneia commentaries. | He said he had taken a graduate course on Job decades earlier but could no longer remember the specific works he used. For a starting point, he suggested checking the NICOT volume because that series is strong in Old Testament work, and possibly Hermeneia for well-done liberal scholarship, while noting neither was a remembered personal bibliography. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=5571" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:32:51</a> | If parents enable the rebellious drunkard in Deuteronomy 21:18-21, is there a punishment for them? | He said the law places the burden on the parents to bring the adult son forward, and the text does not specify a legal penalty for bad parenting beyond communal shame and pressure. | He emphasized that the passage concerns an adult son whose gluttony and drunkenness threaten a community living close to subsistence, not a small child. Because the parents are the ones required to seize and bring him to the elders, the text puts responsibility on them, but it does not prescribe a formal penalty for enabling beyond shame, public pressure, and likely communal consequences. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=6261" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:44:21</a> | Could the Hebrew word for leech in Proverbs 30:15 refer to a vampire because of a later Arabic cognate? | He said no; the proverb uses the leech image for someone who preys on the needy, and reading the later Arabic vampire sense back into Hebrew would be an etymological fallacy. | He read Proverbs 30 as comparing an exploitative person to a literal leech that drains others, especially the afflicted and needy. Later Arabic use of a cognate for a vampire may make sense as a development from the leech image, but he said projecting that later meaning back into Proverbs would misuse etymology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=6635" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:50:35</a> | Does the story of the Eloquent Peasant show materialistic thinking in Egyptian culture? | He said it is less a critique of materialism than a story about status, wisdom, and not overlooking an outsider with real skill. | He said the story belongs to a context without money in the modern sense and should not be read as a straightforward modern critique of materialism. He framed it more as a story about a socially marginal but wise and skilled figure whose worth should not be missed in a status-conscious society. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=6947" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:55:47</a> | How similar is Coptic to ancient Egyptian? | He said Coptic is essentially the last phase of Egyptian, mostly late Egyptian with some Greek vocabulary and a Greek-based script. | He described Coptic as about ninety percent late Egyptian and ten percent Greek vocabulary, written with the Greek alphabet plus additional letters for Egyptian sounds. Because its underlying grammar and syntax are Egyptian, he thought it should ideally be taught after late Egyptian by instructors who understand Egyptian, not only Greek. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=7185" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:59:45</a> | If classical theology says God is dispassionate, how should biblical language about God's emotions be understood? | He said God's emotions are better understood as positional responses, not changes of state, and he was cautious about treating all such language as anthropomorphism. | He said classical theology often treats emotion as a change of state, which creates problems for divine simplicity, but biblical language can be understood as God's unchanging position toward things. For example, God is always against sin and always loves the image of God in people, so anger or love need not imply that God changes internally. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=7892" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:11:32</a> | How did Egyptian warfare develop from the early to late kingdoms in weapons, tactics, and goals? | He traced a progression from stone maces and simple bows to bronze weapons, siege equipment, chariots, composite bows, and later iron and Greek-style arms. | He summarized early warfare as stone maces, simple bows, reed arrows, and flint-edged weapons, with bronze weapons, spears, javelins, and siege equipment becoming more important in the Middle Kingdom. The Second Intermediate Period brought chariots and composite bows, while later periods added more iron, Greek-style arms, and even elephants as mobile archery platforms. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=8594" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:23:14</a> | Why is there no documented evidence of Yahweh before the Bronze Age collapse? | He said preservation and the ambiguity of the divine name are issues, and that Israel as a nation and Israelite Yahweh worship belong to the Exodus and later context. | He said Yahweh is difficult to identify in fragmentary texts because the form can also relate to the verb "to be," unlike more easily recognizable divine names. He also argued that there was no cohesive Israel before the Exodus and that Moses brought Yahweh worship into the people, so one should not expect pre-collapse evidence of Israelites in Egypt worshiping Yahweh. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=9307" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:35:07</a> | Was there a title he wanted instead of *The Ark of the Covenant in Its Egyptian Context*? | He said the working title was *Before the Ark of the Covenant: Exploring the Egyptian Context of Israelite Ritual Furniture*, but the editors' title was better for marketing. | He found the original proposal title and said the working title was longer and more descriptive, but he thought the editors chose a stronger market-facing title. He also mentioned pushing back on other editorial issues, especially keeping dates in the book because the Egyptian material is tied to reigns and chronology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=9704" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:41:44</a> | What is his favorite poetic passage in the Bible, and why? | He chose Psalm 1 for its compact use of Hebrew poetic features, contrast, figurative language, and advice about meditating on Scripture. | He read Psalm 1 as a compact example of Israelite poetry, with couplets, contrast between righteous and wicked, figurative language, and the tree-by-water image. He especially liked that it connects meditation on Scripture with fruitfulness in season rather than immediate payoff. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=9918" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:45:18</a> | What is the oldest Psalm in the Bible? | He said Psalm 90 is probably the oldest, traditionally and textually attributed to Moses. | He identified Psalm 90 because it is introduced as a prayer of Moses and is traditionally, as well as textually, associated with him. He acknowledged that some question Mosaic authorship, but still treated it as probably the oldest psalm in the Psalter. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=10019" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:46:59</a> | What is his favorite evidence for the Trinity? | He cited Isaiah 44:6 as evidence for two persons called Yahweh and Acts 5 as evidence that the Holy Spirit is a person and God, plus a philosophical argument from God's inherent goodness. | He said no single passage gives the whole doctrine, but Isaiah 44:6 shows, in his reading, two distinct persons called Yahweh speaking in unity. He paired that with Acts 5, where lying to the Holy Spirit is lying to God, and added a philosophical argument that divine goodness is more coherent if there are interpersonal relations within the one God before creation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=10527" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:55:27</a> | Why is there a large disproportion of secular liberal or mainline Protestant scholars in biblical academia and fewer evangelical scholars? | He said evangelicals are numerous but have less control of major institutions, presses, journals, and conference resources, so their voice is less amplified. | He argued that evangelical scholars are not absent and may even be numerous, but they are often outside the most prestigious schools, presses, journals, and conference structures. Because liberal or mainline institutions have larger platforms, travel funding, and publication control, their voice can seem much more dominant. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=11080" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:04:40</a> | What did ancient Israelite kings dress like, what did royal garments look like, and did they wear crowns? | He said they wore robes, sandals, and a turban or headcloth with an ornate headband crown, with rosette-patterned fabrics also attested. | He described the royal crown as a headband-like ornament, likely gold for kings, worn over a turban or cloth rather than a modern pointed crown. Based on depictions such as Jehu on the Black Obelisk, he pictured robes from neck to feet, sandals, and decorated fabrics, with rosette or flower patterns appearing on Israelite clothing. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=11519" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:11:59</a> | Was Joseph's colorful cloth common or something very special? | He said colorful tunics were common among Levantine Asiatics, but Joseph's particular pattern would have been distinctive. | He said colorful tunics are attested for Levantine Asiatics, including in scenes such as Beni Hasan, so the presence of a colorful garment was not unique to Joseph. What would distinguish Joseph's garment was the particular woven pattern, since handmade fabrics could carry family or individually recognizable designs. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=11722" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:15:22</a> | Did ancient Egyptians have coffee? | He said no; coffee requires high elevations, and Egyptians did not drink hot drinks until the Islamic period. | He said ancient Egypt did not grow coffee because coffee requires high elevations that Egypt lacks. He connected coffee drinking in Egypt to the Islamic period, when alcohol restrictions created a stronger role for coffee, and added that ancient Egyptians drank beer rather than hot drinks. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=11813" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:16:53</a> | In 2 Chronicles 35, what does Pharaoh Necho mean when he tells Josiah that God is on his side? | He said Necho likely claimed an oracle from a deity, though Chronicles may frame it as God, and Josiah wrongly ignored the warning. | He noted that Necho was a polytheist, but Egyptians could still speak of "God" in the singular when describing an oracle. Necho was on his way to Carchemish to aid Assyria against Babylon and warned Josiah not to interfere; the speaker thought Josiah failed by acting without proper consultation and ignoring a warning that Chronicles presents as divinely significant. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=12246" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:24:06</a> | Why have evangelicals or non-liberals not worked from within institutions and journals to influence academia? | He said some have tried, but institutional culture, tenure pressure, career penalties, and entrenched momentum make internal change very difficult. | He said academic departments have strong cultures and hierarchies, and openly Christian scholars can face tenure, promotion, scheduling, committee, and publication penalties. Even when someone gets tenure, years of ducking conflict can create habits that make later institutional change difficult, so attempts to shift major institutions from inside often have poor returns. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=12627" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:30:27</a> | Has the battle of Gog in Ezekiel 38-39 already taken place, is it yet to come, or is it symbolic? | He said it has not already happened, belongs to apocalyptic literature about the last rebellion, and may be symbolic or a real future battle. | He treated Ezekiel 38-39 as apocalyptic literature about mankind's final rebellion against God, so he rejected attempts to identify it with current wars or past events. He said it is still future, but whether it depicts a literal battle or symbolically represents a final rebellion cannot yet be known because apocalyptic literature reveals itself through later recontextualization. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=13074" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:37:54</a> | Did ancient Egyptians likely consider frogs to be a type of fish? | He said they did not consider frogs fish, though they could view tadpoles that way and associated frogs with magical transformation. | He said the relevant Egyptian text is poetic rather than a biological classification. Egyptians did not consider mature frogs to be fish, but tadpoles could be treated as fish-like, and the transformation from tadpole to frog helped explain the frog's association with magic and the goddess Heqet. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=13267" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:41:07</a> | If Yahweh is connected to the Hebrew verb "to be," why does the form differ from `hayah`? | He said Yahweh reflects a secondary tense of the verb "to be," likely older or Amorite-related, later reserved for the divine name. | He said Hebrew appears to preserve two forms or tenses of the verb "to be," one of which became reserved for the divine name. He suggested the Yahweh form may have originated as an older or Amorite-related secondary tense that later fell out of ordinary Classical Hebrew use, while its connection to `hayah` remains widely recognized. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=13495" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:44:55</a> | Is there support for the conquest of Canaan involving Exodus Israelites joining local Hapiru groups? | He said there is not much evidence for that; it is conceivable but remains speculative. | He said the closest biblical analogue would be groups such as the Gibeonites joining Israel, but they are not Hapiru. Since Hapiru are usually outcasts or mercenaries in the Amarna-period evidence, he allowed the idea as conceivable but said there is no real evidence for Exodus Israelites joining local Hapiru groups in the conquest. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=13628" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:47:08</a> | Could Akhenaten's monotheism have been inspired by Israelites in Egypt? | He said no, because Akhenaten is too early for the Exodus chronology and his Atenism was not true monotheism. | He said Akhenaten belongs in the 1340s BC while the Exodus, in his chronology, is around the 1260s, leaving roughly eighty to ninety years between them. He also said Atenism was better described as monolatrous pantheism than true monotheism, and that Israelites in Egypt before Moses were not yet Yahweh-worshiping monotheists. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/RQKbcY5k4rE?t=13792" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:49:52</a> | Was Revelation coded for its time to evade Nero, and how much of it is prophetic? | He was not persuaded by a strictly Nero-preterist reading, saying Revelation's imagery is heavily Old Testament and 666 may allude to Solomon as a type for a fast-rising ruler. | He allowed that Nero could function as a type of antichrist but was not persuaded that Revelation is mainly a coded Nero text, especially because he dates Nero before the book's setting. He emphasized that Revelation is saturated with Old Testament symbolism and noted that 666 appears with Solomon's gold in 1 Kings 10:14 and 2 Chronicles 9:13, suggesting a symbolic pattern of rapid royal ascent and power. |
